args.command:
python3 -c "
import json
p='reference/current_adaevolve_run/adaevolve_iteration_stats.jsonl'
import os
if not os.path.exists(p):
    print('no file'); exit()
rows=[json.loads(l) for l in open(p)]
for r in rows[-10:]:
    it=r.get('iteration')
    g=r.get('global',{})
    ch=r.get('iteration_result',{}).get('child_program',{}).get('metrics',{})
    print(it, round(g.get('global_best_score',0),4), round(g.get('current_iteration_best_score',0),4), round(ch.get('combined_score',0),4))
"

No web results. From established amyloid kinetics (Knowles/Meisl), the half-time scales as t½ ∝ m0^(-γ) with γ>0, and seeding shortens the lag. Both monomer and seed accelerate the burst onset. Program 3 captured the monomer scaling and was the best (0.8941). The current parent uses only seed in half_sat and scored worse.

The most justified change: revert to Program 3's monomer-dependent half_sat (the proven best), then add a small seed term to the half_sat as well to capture seed-driven lag shortening — both monomer and seed push the inflection earlier. This is one extra constant, well within budget (currently 11). I'll keep it at 11 by reusing.

No singularities, fractional powers, logs, or unbounded growth — the RHS remains a smooth, globally-defined rational function suitable for least-squares from a single start.
